title: Learn AI by Building Real Applications.
subtitle: Build AI. Understand AI. Share AI.

steps.title: 🚀 Learn AI by Building Real Applications
steps.body:
# 📖 Welcome to ABITA

**ABITA** is a visual AI learning platform where you don't just learn about AI...

👉 **You build it.**

Instead of only reading lessons, you'll create **real AI systems** step by step.

steps.title: 🚀 Learn by Doing with ABITA
steps.body:
Unlike traditional courses, you won't just watch videos or read lessons.

With **ABITA**, you'll learn by building **real AI applications**.

Every lesson becomes an **interactive application** that you can **export and share**.

Every chapter includes hands-on activities where you'll:


- 🛠️ Build
- 🧪 Experiment
- 🤖 Train AI models
- 📊 Test your AI
- 🚀 Export and share your applications

steps.title: 🛠️ Every Lesson Becomes a Real Application
steps.body:
As you progress through the curriculum, you'll build exciting **AI-powered applications**.


# 🚀 Projects You'll Build

- 🤖 AI that recognises **Cats and Dogs**
- 📧 AI that detects **Spam Messages**
- 🚦 AI that recognises **Cars and Bikes**
- 🦁 AI that identifies **Wild Animals**
- 🍎 AI that sorts **Fruits**
- 🌱 **Smart Greenhouse** Automation
- 🏫 **Smart Classroom** Controller
- 🚪 **Smart Security** System
- 😊 AI that understands **Positive and Negative Text**
- 💬 Your own **AI Chat Application**


Every new chapter teaches you a **new AI skill**, and every skill helps you build **smarter AI applications**.

steps.title: 🎯 Your Goal
steps.body:
By the end of this journey, you won't just know **what AI is**...

You'll know **how AI systems are built**, because **you'll have built them yourself**.

steps.title: 👋 Welcome to the AI Literacy Journey
steps.body:
Artificial Intelligence is changing the world, but before we can build AI systems, we first need to understand how they work.

In this curriculum, you will learn AI step by step, from the very beginning to modern AI systems.

steps.title: 💡What You Will Learn
steps.body:
# 🧠 Human Intelligence vs Artificial Intelligence



Discover what intelligence is and how humans learn, think, solve problems, and make decisions.

Explore how Artificial Intelligence was inspired by human intelligence and compare how humans and machines solve problems differently.





# 📖 The First AI Systems: Rule-Based AI



Learn how early AI systems worked using rules such as **IF**, **AND**, **OR**, and **NOT**.

🧪 **Build Rule-Based Systems in ABITA**

Create smart systems such as alarms, doors, fans, lights, and automated rooms using rules.





# 📖 Why Rule-Based AI Has Limitations



Discover the challenges of rule-based systems and why they struggle with complex real-world problems.

🧪 **Test the Limits of Rule-Based AI**

Build rule-based systems in **ABITA** and explore situations where rules start to fail.





# 📖 Modern AI: Machine Learning



Learn how modern AI systems learn from examples instead of following fixed rules.

Understand why Machine Learning became the foundation of today's AI systems.

🧪 **Train AI Models in ABITA**

Teach AI systems by providing examples instead of writing rules.





# 📖 Data and AI Models



Learn how data helps AI discover patterns and make predictions.

Explore different types of data and understand how AI models learn from them.

🧪 **Build and Improve AI Models**

Create datasets, train image models, train text models, test their performance, and improve their accuracy.

steps.title: 🎯 Learn by Doing
steps.body:
Throughout this curriculum, you will find:

- **Mini Lessons** to build your understanding of AI concepts.
- **Guided Labs** that show you exactly how to use AI tools in ABITA.
- **Challenge Labs** where you apply your knowledge independently.



By the end of this journey, you will not only understand how AI works, but you will also be able to **collect data, train models, test AI systems, and improve their performance using ABITA**.

steps.title: 🤖 Your Journey into AI
steps.body:
Every AI system was inspired by intelligence.
Let's start by exploring what intelligence really is.


🚀 See you in Chapter 1: Understanding Intelligence.

steps.title: 🧠 What is Intelligence?
steps.body:
**Intelligence** is the ability to:

📚 **Learn**

🤔 **Think**

✅ **Make Decisions**




There are **two types of intelligence** we will learn about in this course:

🧠 **Human Intelligence**

🤖 **Artificial Intelligence (AI)**




Let's explore both!

steps.title: 🧠 Human Intelligence
steps.body:
Human Intelligence helps us:

📚 **Learn from Experience**

🤔 **Think About Situations**

✅ **Make Decisions**


Let's see how humans use intelligence every day.






## 📚 Learn from Experience

Imagine you are riding your bicycle.

🚲 One day, you hit a small obstacle and almost fall.

The next day, when you see the same obstacle, you go around it.

👉 **You learned from your experience.**







## 🤔 Think About Situations

Imagine it is raining outside.

🌧️ Before leaving home, you think:

- **Should I take an umbrella?**
- **What if it rains more?**
- **Will I get wet?**

After thinking about the situation, you prepare accordingly.

👉 **You used intelligence to think before acting.**







## ✅ Make Decisions 

Imagine you have:

📖 **Homework to complete**

🎮 **A game you want to play**

You decide to finish your homework first and play later.

👉 **You made a decision.**

steps.title: 🌟 Human Intelligence Summary
steps.body:
Every day, humans use intelligence to:

📚 **Learn from Experience**

🤔 **Think About Situations**

✅ **Make Decisions**

Whether we are studying, playing sports, or solving problems, **we use intelligence all the time.**

steps.title: 🤖 Artificial Intelligence (AI)
steps.body:
**Artificial Intelligence (AI)** is intelligence created by humans.

AI can:

📋 **Follow Instructions**

📚 **Learn from Data**

✅ **Make Decisions or Predictions**


Let's see how AI works.



---



## 1. 📋 Follow Instructions

Imagine you tell a robot:

**"If the room is dark, turn on the light."**

The robot follows your instruction.

👉 **It is doing exactly what you told it to do.**







## 2.  📚 Learn from Data

Imagine an AI sees thousands of pictures of cats.

🐱 🐱 🐱 🐱

Over time, it learns what cats usually look like.

👉 **It learned from data.**







## 3. ✅ Make Decisions or Predictions

Imagine you watch cricket videos every day on YouTube.

🏏 🏏 🏏

Soon, YouTube starts recommending more cricket videos.



👉 **The AI predicts what you might like to watch.**

steps.title: 🌟 Artificial Intelligence Summary
steps.body:
AI can:

📋 **Follow Instructions**

📚 **Learn from Data**

✅ **Make Decisions or Predictions**


AI can perform some tasks that appear intelligent, but it is created by humans.

steps.title: 🚀 Why Do We Build AI?
steps.body:
The goal of AI is to **mimic some parts of human intelligence**.

Why?

⚡ **To Save Time**

📈 **To Improve Efficiency**

🔍 **To Process Large Amounts of Information**

🤝 **To Help Humans Solve Problems**





## 🌟 Remember

🧠 **Humans create AI.**

🤖 **AI helps humans.**

👉 **AI is a tool designed to help us solve problems and make life easier.**

steps.title: 🤔 How Does AI Know What To Do?
steps.body:
We learned that AI can:


- 📋 **Follow Instructions**

- 📚 **Learn from Data**

- ✅ **Make Decisions or Predictions**




| 🧠 Human Intelligence | 🤖 Artificial Intelligence (AI) |
| --- | --- |
| Learns from Experience | Learns from Data |
| Thinks About Situations | Follows Instructions |
| Makes Decisions | Makes Decisions or Predictions |




But who teaches AI what to do?

👉 Humans do!

AI follows instructions called **rules**.



🌟 In the next chapter, we will learn how to teach computers using rules.

steps.title: A Quick History of AI
steps.body:
In 1956, scientists started exploring how computers could become intelligent.

One of their first ideas was simple: teach computers to follow rules.



For example:

- If a **Switch** is ON → Turn the **Bulb** ON.
- If **Motion** is detected → Turn the **Fan** ON.
- If the **Temperature** is above **35°C** → Turn the **AC** ON.


This approach became known as **Rule-Based AI**.



In this chapter, you will build your own Rule-Based AI systems using ABITA.

steps.title: 💡Turn On the Bulb
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to connect an input to an action.



## Problem
If the **switch** is **ON**, the **bulb** should turn **ON**.


## Instructions

**Step 1:**
Drag **Switch** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 2:**
Drag **Bulb** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 3:**
Connect **Switch** to **Bulb**.

**Step 4:**
Turn **ON** the **switch** and observe the result.




## Expected Result

✅ When the **switch** is **ON**, the **bulb** turns **ON**.
✅ When the **switch** is **OFF**, the **bulb** turns **OFF**.




## What You Learned

1. Inputs can control actions.
2. Components can be connected together to create a simple smart system.

steps.title: 🌀Turn On the Fan
steps.instruction:
## Problem

If the **Switch** is **ON**, the **Fan** should turn **ON**.




## Instructions

**Step 1:**
Drag **Switch** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 2:**
Drag **Fan** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 3:**
Connect **Switch** to **Fan**.

**Step 4:**
Turn **ON** the **Switch** and observe the result.

**Step 5:**
Turn **OFF** the **Switch** and observe the result.





## Expected Result

✅ When the **Switch** is **ON**, the **Fan** turns **ON**.

✅ When the **Switch** is **OFF**, the **Fan** turns **OFF**.





## What You Learned

1. A **Switch** can control different devices.

2. Inputs can be connected directly to actions to build simple smart systems.

steps.title: 🚪Open the Door
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to control different devices using the same input.


## Problem

If the **switch** is **ON**, the **door** should **open**.




## Instructions

**Step 1: ** Drag **Switch** from the **Rule-Based menu**.
**Step 2:** Drag **Door** from the **Actions** menu.
**Step 3:** Connect **Switch** to **Door**.
**Step 4:** Turn **ON** the **switch** and observe the result.




## Expected Result

✅ When the **switch** is **ON**, the **door** opens.
✅ When the **switch** is **OFF**, the **door** closes.




## What You Learned

1. The same **input** can control different **actions**.
2. Computers can automate devices such as **lights**, **fans**, and **doors**.

steps.title: Challenge Lab 1: Smart Classroom Starter
steps.prompt:
**Problem**

A teacher wants the classroom to be ready when students arrive.

Turn ON both the **Bulb** and **Fan** using a single **Switch**.

---

**Requirements**

- Use one **Switch**.
- Use a **Bulb**.
- Use a **Fan**.

---

**Success Criteria**

✅ Both the **Bulb** and **Fan** turn ON when the **Switch** is ON.

✅ Both devices turn OFF when the **Switch** is OFF.

steps.title: 🚶Motion Light
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to use an **IF Condition** with a **Motion Sensor**.




## Problem

If **motion** is detected, turn the **bulb** ON.




## Instructions

- Drag **Motion Sensor** from the **Rule-Based menu**.
- Drag **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.
- Drag **Bulb** from the **Actions** menu.
- Connect **Motion Sensor** to the **IF Condition**.
- Connect the **IF Condition** to the **Bulb**.
- Double-click the **IF Condition**.
- Set the condition to **Motion Detected**.
- Double-click the **Motion Sensor**.
- Click the **No Motion** button to change its state.
- Observe the result.




## Expected Result

✅ When **Motion** is detected, the **bulb** turns **ON**.
✅ When **No Motion** is detected, the **bulb** remains **OFF**.




**What You Learned**

1. An **IF Condition** helps computers make decisions.
2. Sensors provide information that can be used in rules.
steps.canvasHint: Click on a block to select it, its settings appear in the Properties panel on the right.

steps.title: 5. 🌬️Smart Fan
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how the same **IF Condition** can control different devices.




## Problem

If **motion** is detected, turn the **fan** ON.




## Instructions

- Drag **Motion Sensor** from the **Rule-Based menu**.
- Drag **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.
- Drag **Fan** from the **Actions** menu.
- Connect **Motion Sensor** to the **IF Condition**.
- Connect the **IF Condition** to the **Fan**.
- Double-click the **IF Condition**.
- Set the condition to **Motion Detected**.
- Double-click the **Motion Sensor**.

- Observe the result.




## Expected Result

✅ When **Motion** is detected, the **fan** turns **ON**.
✅ When **No Motion** is detected, the **fan** remains **OFF**.




## What You Learned

2. Rules can be reused to automate many actions.

steps.title: Challenge Lab 2: Smart Entrance Door
steps.prompt:
## Problem

A building has an automatic entrance door.

Open the **Door** when **Motion** is detected near the entrance.



## Requirements

- Use a **Motion Sensor**
- Use an **IF Condition**
- Use a **Door**



## Success Criteria

- ✅ The Door opens when Motion is detected
- ✅ The Door remains closed when there is No Motion

steps.title: Challenge Lab 3: Welcome Home System
steps.prompt:
**Problem**

Turn ON both the **Bulb** and **Fan** when **Motion** is detected.



**Requirements**

- Use a **Motion Sensor**.
- Use an **IF Condition**.
- Use a **Bulb**.
- Use a **Fan**.



**Success Criteria**

✅ Both the **Bulb** and **Fan** turn **ON** when **Motion** is detected.

✅ Both devices remain **OFF** when there is **No Motion**.

steps.title: 🥵Hot Room Alert
steps.instruction:
## Problem

If the **temperature** is greater than **35°C**, turn the **fan** ON.




## Instructions

- Drag **Temperature Sensor** from the **Rule-Based menu**.
- Drag **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.
- Drag **Fan** from the **Actions** menu.
- Connect **Temperature Sensor** to the **IF Condition**.
- Connect the **IF Condition** to the **Fan**.
- Double-click the **IF Condition**.
- Set the condition to **Temperature > 35°C**.
- Double-click the **Temperature Sensor**.
- Move the slider to **30°C** and observe the result.
- Move the slider to **36°C** and observe the result.




## Expected Result

✅ When the **temperature** is **30°C**, the **fan** remains **OFF**.
✅ When the **temperature** is **36°C**, the **fan** turns **ON**.




## What You Learned

- An **IF Condition** can compare values.
- Computers can make decisions based on sensor readings.

steps.title: ❄️Hot Room Cooling System
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how one **IF Condition** can control multiple devices.



## Problem

If the **temperature** is greater than **40°C**, turn **ON** the **Fan** and the **AC**.



## Instructions


**Step 1:**
Drag **Temperature Sensor** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 2:**
Drag **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 3:**
Drag **Fan** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 4:**
Drag **AC** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 5:**
Connect **Temperature Sensor** to the **IF Condition**.

**Step 6:**
Connect the **IF Condition** to the **Fan**.

**Step 7:**
Connect the **IF Condition** to the **AC**.

**Step 8:**
Double-click the **IF Condition**.

**Step 9:**
Set the condition to **Temperature > 40°C**.

**Step 10:**
Double-click the **Temperature Sensor**.

**Step 11:**
Move the slider to **35°C** and observe the result.

**Step 12:**
Move the slider to **41°C** and observe the result.





## Expected Result

✅ When the **temperature** is **35°C**, the **Fan** and **AC** remain **OFF**.

✅ When the **temperature** is **41°C**, the **Fan** and **AC** turn **ON**.





## What You Learned

1. One **IF Condition** can control multiple devices.
2. A single rule can trigger multiple actions.

steps.title: 🔑Password Protected Door
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to use **Text Input** with an **IF Condition**.




## Problem

If the password is **Pakistan**, open the **Door**.




## Instructions


**Step 1:**
Drag **Text Input** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 2:**
Drag **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 3:**
Drag **Door** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 4:**
Connect **Text Input** to the **IF Condition**.

**Step 5:**
Connect the **IF Condition** to the **Door**.

**Step 6:**
Double-click the **IF Condition**.

**Step 7:**
Set the condition to **Text = Pakistan**.

**Step 8:**
Double-click the **Text Input**.

**Step 9:**
Enter **pakistan** and observe the result.

**Step 10:**
Enter **Pakistan** and observe the result.






## Expected Result

✅ When the text is **pakistan**, the **Door** remains **closed**.

✅ When the text is **Pakistan**, the **Door** opens.





## What You Learned

1. Text can be used as an input for rules.

2. An **IF Condition** can compare words as well as numbers.

3. Text matching is **case-sensitive**, so **Pakistan** and **pakistan** are treated as different values.

steps.title: 👋Smart Greeting Detector
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to use the **Contains** condition with **Text Input**.




## Problem

If the text contains the word **hello**, turn the **Bulb** ON.




## Instructions

**Step 1:**
Drag **Text Input** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 2:**
Drag **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 3:**
Drag **Bulb** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 4:**
Connect **Text Input** to the **IF Condition**.

**Step 5:**
Connect the **IF Condition** to the **Bulb**.

**Step 6:**
Double-click the **IF Condition** to edit it.

**Step 7:**
From the **Condition** dropdown, select **Contains**.

**Step 8:**
In the **Value to compare** field, type **hello**.

**Step 9:**
Double-click the **Text Input** component.

**Step 10:**
Enter **Good morning everyone** and observe the result.

**Step 11:**
Enter **hello everyone** and observe the result.




## Expected Result

✅ When the text is **Good morning everyone**, the **Bulb** remains **OFF**.

✅ When the text is **hello everyone**, the **Bulb** turns **ON**.




## What You Learned

1. The **Contains** condition can search for a word inside a sentence.

2. A sentence can trigger an action even when it contains additional words.

3. Text matching is **case-sensitive**, so **hello** and **Hello** are treated as different values.

steps.title: 🏆 Challenge Lab 4: VIP Event Access
steps.prompt:
## Problem

A VIP event is taking place.

Only guests who enter the VIP Code **78601** should be allowed to enter.

Build a system that grants access only when the correct code is entered.



## Requirements

- Use **Text Input**
- Use an **IF Condition**
- Use a **Door**



## Success Criteria

- ✅ The Door opens when the text is **78601**
- ✅ The Door remains closed for any other text



## Expected Result

🤔 Many security systems use access codes to control entry and protect restricted areas.

steps.title: 🎚️Two Switch AC System
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to use an **AND Condition**.





## Problem

Turn the **AC** ON only when **both switches** are ON.




## Instructions


**Step 1:**
Drag **Switch** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 2:**
Drag another **Switch** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 3:**
Drag **AND Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 4:**
Drag **AC** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 5:**
Connect the first **Switch** to the **AND Condition**.

**Step 6:**
Connect the second **Switch** to the **AND Condition**.

**Step 7:**
Connect the **AND Condition** to the **AC**.

**Step 8:**
Turn ON only the first **Switch** and observe the result.

**Step 9:**
Turn ON only the second **Switch** and observe the result.

**Step 10:**
Turn ON both **Switches** and observe the result.





## Expected Result

✅ When only one **Switch** is ON, the **AC** remains **OFF**.

✅ When both **Switches** are ON, the **AC** turns **ON**.





## What You Learned

1. An **AND Condition** requires all conditions to be true.

2. The output turns ON only when both inputs are ON.

steps.title: 🏆 Challenge Lab 5: Smart Security Door
steps.prompt:
## Problem

Build a security system that unlocks the **Door** only when:

- Switch A is ON
- Switch B is ON

Both switches represent two security approvals.

The door should only open when both approvals have been given.



## Requirements

- Use **2 Switches**
- Use **1 AND Condition**
- Use **1 Door**



## Success Criteria

- ✅ The Door remains CLOSED when only one Switch is ON
- ✅ The Door opens when both Switches are ON
- ✅ The Door closes again if either Switch is turned OFF

steps.title: 💡Backup Light System
steps.instruction:
## Goal
  
Learn how to use an **OR Condition**.



## Problem
  
A room has **two switches**.

The **Light** should turn ON if **either Switch A OR Switch B** is ON.

This way, if one switch fails, the other switch can still turn the Light ON.





## Instructions

**Step 1:**  
Drag **Switch** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 2:**  
Drag another **Switch** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 3:**  
Drag **OR Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 4:**  
Drag **Bulb** from the **Actions menu**.

**Step 5:**  
Connect the first **Switch** to the **OR Condition**.

**Step 6:**  
Connect the second **Switch** to the **OR Condition**.

**Step 7:**  
Connect the **OR Condition** to the **Bulb**.

**Step 8:**  
Turn ON only the first **Switch** and observe the result.

**Step 9:**  
Turn ON only the second **Switch** and observe the result.

**Step 10:**  
Turn OFF both **Switches** and observe the result.





## Expected Result

✅ When the first **Switch** is ON, the **Bulb** turns **ON**.

✅ When the second **Switch** is ON, the **Bulb** turns **ON**.

✅ When both **Switches** are ON, the **Bulb** turns **ON**.

❌ When both **Switches** are OFF, the **Bulb** remains **OFF**.





## What You Learned

1. An **OR Condition** requires at least one condition to be true.

2. The output turns **ON** when either input is **ON**.

3. **OR Conditions** are useful for creating backup systems.

steps.title: 🏆 Challenge Lab 6: Emergency Cooling System
steps.prompt:
## Problem

An emergency cooling system has two emergency switches.

If either switch is activated, the cooling fan should start immediately.

Build a system that turns ON the **Fan** if either of these conditions is true:

- Switch A is ON
- Switch B is ON



## Requirements

- Use **2 Switches**
- Use **1 OR Condition**
- Use **1 Fan**



## Success Criteria

- ✅ The Fan turns ON when Switch A is ON
- ✅ The Fan turns ON when Switch B is ON
- ✅ The Fan turns ON when both Switches are ON
- ✅ The Fan remains OFF when both Switches are OFF

steps.title: 🛡️Security Door
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to use a **NOT Condition**.





## Problem

Open the **Door** only when the **Switch** is OFF.



## Instructions

**Step 1:**
Drag **Switch** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 2:**
Drag **NOT Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 3:**
Drag **Door** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 4:**
Connect the **Switch** to the **NOT Condition**.

**Step 5:**
Connect the **NOT Condition** to the **Door**.

**Step 6:**
Turn the **Switch** OFF and observe the result.

**Step 7:**
Turn the **Switch** ON and observe the result.




## Expected Result

✅ When the **Switch** is **OFF**, the **Door** opens.

✅ When the **Switch** is **ON**, the **Door** remains closed.





## What You Learned

1. A **NOT Condition** reverses a result.

2. If something is **TRUE**, **NOT** makes it **FALSE**.

3. If something is **FALSE**, **NOT** makes it **TRUE**.

steps.title: 🏆 Challenge Lab 7: Night Light
steps.prompt:
## Problem

A night light should automatically turn ON when the room switch is OFF.

Build a system that turns ON the **Bulb** when the **Switch** is OFF.



## Requirements

- Use **1 Switch**
- Use **1 NOT Condition**
- Use **1 Bulb**



## Success Criteria

- ✅ The Bulb turns ON when the Switch is OFF
- ✅ The Bulb turns OFF when the Switch is ON

steps.title: 🚨Motion + Switch Security Light
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to combine multiple conditions using an **AND Condition**.





## Problem


Build a security light that turns ON only when:

- Motion is detected
- Switch A is ON

The Bulb should remain OFF if either condition is not true.





## Instructions


**Step 1:**
Drag a **Motion Sensor** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 2:**
Drag an **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 3:**
Connect the **Motion Sensor** to the **IF Condition**.

**Step 4:**
Configure the **IF Condition** to detect:

**Motion Detected**

**Step 5:**
Drag a **Switch** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 6:**
Drag an **AND Condition** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 7:**
Drag a **Bulb** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 8:**
Connect the **IF Condition** to the **AND Condition**.

**Step 9:**
Connect the **Switch** to the **AND Condition**.

**Step 10:**
Connect the **AND Condition** to the **Bulb**.

**Step 11:**
Turn ON the Switch only and observe the result.

**Step 12:**
Trigger Motion only and observe the result.

**Step 13:**
Turn ON the Switch and trigger Motion at the same time.

**Step 14:**
Observe the result.





## Success Criteria


- ✅ The Bulb remains OFF when only Motion is detected
- ✅ The Bulb remains OFF when only the Switch is ON
- ✅ The Bulb turns ON when both conditions are TRUE





## What You Learned


1. An **AND Condition** can combine different types of conditions.
2. All connected conditions must be TRUE for the output to activate.
3. Sensors and switches can work together to create smarter systems.

steps.title: 🚪Secure Server Room
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to combine more than two conditions using multiple **AND Conditions**.



## Problem

A secure server room can only be opened when:

- Switch A is ON
- Switch B is ON
- Switch C is ON

If any switch is OFF, the Door must remain closed.



## Instructions

**Step 1:**

Drag **Switch** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 2:**

Drag another **Switch** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 3:**

Drag a third **Switch** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 4:**

Drag **AND Condition** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 5:**

Drag another **AND Condition** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 6:**

Drag **Door** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 7:**

Connect **Switch A** and **Switch B** to the first **AND Condition**.

**Step 8:**

Connect the output of the first **AND Condition** to the second **AND Condition**.

**Step 9:**

Connect **Switch C** to the second **AND Condition**.

**Step 10:**

Connect the second **AND Condition** to the **Door**.

**Step 11:**

Turn ON all three **Switches** and observe the result.

**Step 12:**

Turn OFF any one **Switch** and observe the result.



## Expected Result

- ✅ The Door opens when all three Switches are ON
- ❌ The Door remains closed if any Switch is OFF



## What You Learned

1. Some systems require more than two conditions to be true.

2. Multiple **AND Conditions** can be connected together.

3. The output of one logic block can be used as an input to another logic block.

4. Complex rule-based systems are created by combining simpler rules.

steps.title: Challenge Lab 8: Security Approval System
steps.prompt:
**Problem**

Turn the **AC** ON only when:

- **Switch A** is ON.
- **Switch B** is ON.
- **Motion** is detected.



**Requirements**

- Use **2 Switches**.
- Use a **Motion Sensor**.
- Use **IF Conditions**.
- Use **2** **AND Conditions**.
- Use an **AC**.



**Success Criteria**

✅ The **AC** turns **ON** only when all three conditions are TRUE.

✅ The **AC** remains **OFF** if any condition is FALSE.

steps.title: 🌡️Comfort Zone Temperature Control
steps.instruction:
## Goal
Learn how to combine two **IF Conditions** using an **AND Condition**.




## Problem

Turn the **Fan** ON only when the **temperature** is between **30°C** and **35°C**.





## Instructions

**Step 1:**
Drag **Temperature Sensor** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 2:**
Drag **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 3:**
Drag another **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 4:**
Drag **AND Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 5:**
Drag **Fan** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 6:**
Connect the **Temperature Sensor** to both **IF Conditions**.

**Step 7:**
Connect both **IF Conditions** to the **AND Condition**.

**Step 8:**
Connect the **AND Condition** to the **Fan**.

**Step 9:**
Double-click the first **IF Condition**.

**Step 10:**
Set the condition to **Temperature > 30°C**.

**Step 11:**
Double-click the second **IF Condition**.

**Step 12:**
Set the condition to **Temperature < 35°C**.

**Step 13:**
Double-click the **Temperature Sensor**.

**Step 14:**
Move the slider to **25°C** and observe the result.

**Step 15:**
Move the slider to **32°C** and observe the result.

**Step 16:**
Move the slider to **40°C** and observe the result.






## Expected Result

✅ When the **temperature** is **25°C**, the **Fan** remains **OFF**.

✅ When the **temperature** is **32°C**, the **Fan** turns **ON**.

✅ When the **temperature** is **40°C**, the **Fan** remains **OFF**.






## What You Learned

- Two **IF Conditions** can be combined using an **AND Condition**.

- The **AND Condition** becomes TRUE only when both conditions are TRUE.

- Computers can use ranges to make smarter decisions.

steps.title: 🏠Occupied Room Cooling System
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to combine multiple **IF Conditions** using an **AND Condition**.



## Problem

Turn the **AC** ON only when **motion is detected** and the **temperature** is greater than **35°C**.



## Instructions


**Step 1:**
Drag **Motion Sensor** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 2:**
Drag **Temperature Sensor** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 3:**
Drag **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 4:**
Drag another **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 5:**
Drag **AND Condition** from the **Rule-Based menu**.

**Step 6:**
Drag **AC** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 7:**
Connect the **Motion Sensor** to the first **IF Condition**.

**Step 8:**
Connect the **Temperature Sensor** to the second **IF Condition**.

**Step 9:**
Connect both **IF Conditions** to the **AND Condition**.

**Step 10:**
Connect the **AND Condition** to the **AC**.

**Step 11:**
Double-click the first **IF Condition**.

**Step 12:**
Set the condition to **Motion Detected**.

**Step 13:**
Double-click the second **IF Condition**.

**Step 14:**
Set the condition to **Temperature > 35°C**.

**Step 15:**
Double-click the **Motion Sensor** and set it to **No Motion**.

**Step 16:**
Double-click the **Temperature Sensor** and move the slider to **40°C**.

**Step 17:**
Observe the result.

**Step 18:**
Change the **Motion Sensor** to **Motion Detected**.

**Step 19:**
Observe the result.






## Expected Result


✅ When the **temperature** is **40°C** but there is **No Motion**, the **AC** remains **OFF**.

✅ When **Motion** is detected and the **temperature** is **40°C**, the **AC** turns **ON**.




## What You Learned

Multiple **IF Conditions** can be combined using an **AND Condition**.

The **AND Condition** becomes TRUE only when all connected conditions are TRUE.

Smart systems often use multiple sensors before taking action.

steps.title: 🏆 Challenge Lab 9: Perfect Comfort Room
steps.prompt:
## Problem

A room is considered comfortable only when:

- Temperature is between **30°C and 35°C**
- Humidity is above **70%**

Turn the **Fan** ON only when both conditions are true.



## Requirements

- Use a **Temperature Sensor**
- Use a **Humidity Sensor**
- Use **3 IF Conditions**
- Use **AND Conditions**
- Use a **Fan**



## Success Criteria

- ✅ The Fan turns ON when the temperature is between **30°C and 35°C** and humidity is above **70%**
- ✅ The Fan remains OFF when the temperature is below **30°C**
- ✅ The Fan remains OFF when the temperature is above **35°C**
- ✅ The Fan remains OFF when humidity is **70% or below**



## Expected Result

🤔 Real-world smart systems often combine multiple sensor readings before taking action.

🤔 A single condition is usually not enough.

steps.title: 🏡Smart Energy-Saving Room
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how multiple rules can work together in one smart system.



## Problem

Build a smart room that follows these rules:

- If Motion is detected, turn the **Bulb** ON.
- If the Temperature is between **30°C and 35°C**, turn the **Fan** ON.
- If the Temperature is greater than **35°C**, turn the **AC** ON.



## Requirements

- Use **1 Motion Sensor**
- Use **1 Temperature Sensor**
- Use **4 IF Conditions**
- Use **1 AND Condition**
- Use **1 Bulb**
- Use **1 Fan**
- Use **1 AC**



## Instructions

**Step 1:**
Add the required sensors and devices.

**Step 2:**
Create the rule for the **Bulb**.

**Step 3:**
Create the temperature range for the **Fan**.

**Step 4:**
Create the rule for the **AC**.

**Step 5:**
Test the system using different temperature values.



## Expected Result

- ✅ The Bulb turns ON when Motion is detected
- ✅ The Fan turns ON when the Temperature is between **30°C and 35°C**
- ✅ The AC turns ON when the Temperature is greater than **35°C**
- ✅ All rules work together in the same system



## What You Learned

1. A smart system can contain multiple rules.

2. Different sensors can control different devices.

3. Temperature ranges can be used to make smarter decisions.

4. Complex systems are built by combining simple rules.

steps.title: 🌱 Challenge Lab 10: Smart Greenhouse
steps.prompt:
## Problem

Plants need the right temperature and humidity to grow.

Create a **Smart Greenhouse** that automatically controls the environment.



## Requirements

- If the Temperature is between **30°C and 35°C** → Turn the **Fan** ON
- If the Humidity is below **40%** → Turn the **Alarm** ON
- If the Temperature is greater than **40°C** **AND** the Humidity is below **40%** → Turn the **AC** ON



## Allowed Components

- **1 Temperature Sensor**
- **1 Humidity Sensor**
- **1 Fan**
- **1 AC**
- **1 Alarm**
- Any number of **IF Conditions**
- Any number of **AND, OR, and NOT Conditions**



## Success Criteria

- ✅ The Fan turns ON when the Temperature is between **30°C and 35°C**
- ✅ The Alarm turns ON when the Humidity is below **40%**
- ✅ The AC turns ON only when the Temperature is greater than **40°C** and the Humidity is below **40%**
- ✅ Only the allowed components are used
- ✅ All rules work together correctly



## Expected Result

🤔 Real greenhouses use multiple sensors to monitor environmental conditions and automatically control equipment to keep plants healthy.

🤔 This challenge combines temperature ranges, humidity checks, and multiple rules into a single smart system.

steps.title: 📌Summary
steps.body:
Great job!

In this chapter, you learned that:



📋 **AI follows instructions.**

🔀 **Rules use IF–THEN logic.**

🤖 **AI follows rules exactly as they are written.**

✅ **The same input produces the same output.**

✅ **Rules work well in simple and predictable situations.**




Using ABITA, you built your own **rule-based AI systems** and taught AI how to respond to different situations.



🌟 **You are now ready to explore a bigger question: Can rules solve every problem?**

steps.title: 🤔 When Rules Are Not Enough
steps.body:
In the last chapter, you taught AI using **rules**.

You learned that rules can help AI make decisions.

But can rules solve **every problem**?


Let's find out!

steps.title: ⚠️ What is a Spam Detector?
steps.body:
# 📧 What is a Spam Detector?

Imagine your inbox receives 100 messages today.

Some messages are important:

📧 "Your teacher shared today's homework."

📧 "Your football match starts at 5 PM."





But some messages are unwanted:

📧 "Congratulations! You won a prize!"

📧 "Click here to claim your reward!"

📧 "You have won $1,000,000!"




A Spam Detector helps separate unwanted messages from important ones so we do not miss something important.

steps.title: 🚀 Your Mission
steps.body:
🚀 **Your Mission**

Can you build a spam detector using only **rules**?



👉 In this chapter, we will discover whether simple rules are enough to build a good spam detector.

steps.title: 🚨Detect a Spam Word
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how a rule can be used to detect a spam message.




## Problem

You work for an email company.

Your security team has discovered that many spam emails contain the word:



**MONEY**

Create a spam detector that triggers the Alarm whenever this word appears.




## Instructions


**Step 1:**

Drag a **Text Input** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 2:**

Drag an **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 3:**

Drag an **Alarm** from the **Actions** menu.

**Step 4:**

Connect:

**Text Input → IF Condition**

**IF Condition → Alarm**

**Step 5:**

Double-click the **IF Condition**.

Configure it as:

**Contains: MONEY**

**Step 6:**

Go to the **Text Input** and type:

**MONEY**

Observe the result.

**Step 7:**

Now type:

**mOneY**

Observe the result.

The Alarm should remain silent.



## Success Criteria

✅ Alarm turns ON when the message contains **MONEY**.

✅ Alarm remains OFF for messages that do not contain **MONEY**.

steps.title: 📖 Spammers Found a New Variation
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how rule-based systems need additional rules when new variations appear.




## Problem

Your spam detector successfully detects: **MONEY**

However, spammers have discovered a way to bypass your system.

Instead of writing: **MONEY**

they now write: **MonEy**

Update your spam detector so it can detect both versions.




## Instructions

**Step 1:**

Open your existing spam detector.

**Step 2:**

Drag a new **IF Condition** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 3:**

Drag an **OR Condition** from the **Rule-Based** menu.

**Step 4:**

Connect both **IF Conditions** to the **OR Condition**.

**Step 5:**

Connect the **OR Condition** to the **Alarm**.

**Step 6:**

Double-click the new **IF Condition**.

Configure it as:

**Contains: MonEy**

**Step 7:**

Go to the **Text Input** and type:

**MONEY**

Observe the result.

The Alarm should turn ON.

**Step 8:**

Now type:

**MonEy**

Observe the result.

The Alarm should turn ON.



## Success Criteria

✅ Alarm turns ON when the message contains **MONEY**.

✅ Alarm turns ON when the message contains **MonEy**.

✅ Alarm remains OFF for other messages.




## Expected Result

🤔 Your detector can now catch two variations of the same spam word, but it requires additional rules to do so.

steps.title: Challenge Lab 1: Improve the Spam Detector
steps.prompt:
## Problem

Spammers have created another variation:

**money**

Update your spam detector so it can detect:

- **MONEY**
- **MonEy**
- **money**



## Requirements

- Use the existing spam detector.
- Use an **IF Condition**.
- Use **OR Conditions** if required.
- Use an **Alarm**.



## Success Criteria

✅ Alarm turns ON when the message contains **MONEY**.

✅ Alarm turns ON when the message contains **MonEy**.

✅ Alarm turns ON when the message contains **money**.

✅ Alarm remains OFF for messages that do not contain these words.

steps.title: Challenge Lab 2: Keep Up with the Spammers
steps.prompt:
## Problem

Spammers have created another variation:

**MONey**

Update your spam detector so it can detect this variation as well.



## Requirements

- Use the existing spam detector.
- Use an **IF Condition**.
- Use **OR Conditions**.
- Use an **Alarm**.



## Success Criteria

✅ Alarm turns ON when the message contains **MONEY**.

✅ Alarm turns ON when the message contains **MonEy**.

✅ Alarm turns ON when the message contains **money**.

✅ Alarm turns ON when the message contains **MoNeY**.

✅ Alarm turns ON when the message contains **MONey**.

✅ Alarm remains OFF for messages that do not contain these words.

steps.title: 🧩 The Rule Problem
steps.body:
So far, we have been trying to detect the word:

**MONEY**


At first, this seemed easy.

We created one rule:

**IF Text = MONEY → Alarm**



But then spammers started using different variations:

- **Money**
- **money**
- **MonEy**
- **MoNeY**
- **MONey**
- **mONeY**
- **moneY**


For every new variation, we had to add another rule.

steps.title: 🔄 More Variations, More Rules
steps.body:
## How Many Rules Do We Need?

The word **MONEY** has 5 letters.

Each letter can be **uppercase** or **lowercase**.

This means there are many possible variations.

Just to detect one word, we may need dozens of **IF Conditions**.

And we still might miss some variations.
steps.tip:
The word "MONEY" has 5 letters.

Each letter can be uppercase or lowercase.

This means there are:

2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 = 32 possible variations.

Would you create 32 different IF Conditions just to detect one word?

steps.title: 🚀 The Bigger Word Problem
steps.body:
## What About a Longer Word?

Now imagine we want to detect:

**CONGRATULATIONS**

Possible variations include:

- **Congratulations**
- **CONGRATULATIONS**
- **congratulations**
- **CoNgRaTuLaTiOnS**

And many more.

Would we create a new rule for every variation?

That would require a lot of IF Conditions.
steps.tip:
The word "CONGRATULATIONS" has 15 letters.

That means it can be written in:

"32,768" different ways.

Would you create 32,768 "IF Conditions" for a single word?

steps.title: 💬 The Sentence Problem
steps.body:
## What About Full Sentences?

Now imagine we want to detect this spam message:

**Congratulations! You have won a free prize.**

A spammer could write:

- **Congrats! You won a reward.**
- **Claim your free gift now!**
- **You have been selected for a special offer!**

All of these messages have a similar meaning, but they use different words.

Would we need hundreds or even thousands of rules to detect every possible sentence?

steps.title: 🤯 Now Imagine Detecting an Image...
steps.body:
A single image is **not** made of just a few letters.

It contains **thousands, or even millions, of tiny dots** called **pixels**.

If we tried to write **IF rules** for every possible combination of pixels...

we would need **millions of rules**.

That is simply **not practical**.

steps.title: 🤔 If Rules Can't Do It, What Can?
steps.body:
So...

If we can't write **rules** for every possible image...

how do AI systems recognise **cats, dogs, faces, traffic signs,** or **handwritten numbers**?

There must be **another way**.

👉 In the next chapter, you'll discover how **machines learn from examples** instead of following **thousands of rules**.

steps.title: 📌 Summary
steps.body:
## What We Learned

- Rules can help computers make decisions.
- A spam detector can be created using IF Conditions.
- Every new variation requires additional rules.
- Longer words need even more rules.
- Full sentences can be written in many different ways.
- As rules increase, systems become larger and harder to manage.


📌 Sometimes writing more rules is not the best solution.



🌟 What if we could teach AI without writing thousands of rules?

How would that work?

Let's find out in the next chapter.

steps.title: 🤔 When Rules Are Not Enough
steps.body:
In the last chapter, we discovered a problem.

Our spam detector could recognise:

**MONEY**

But when we changed it to:

**MonEy**

The rule stopped working.

To fix it, we had to create another rule.

Then another.

And another.


🤔 Imagine doing this for thousands of words.

Or millions of messages.

It would be almost impossible.

steps.title: A Big Question
steps.body:
If rules keep breaking...

Is there another way for machines to learn?

Can a machine learn from **examples** instead of being given every rule?

steps.title: The Beginning of Machine Learning
steps.body:
This is where **Machine Learning** begins.

Instead of writing rules ourselves, we give the machine examples.

The machine studies those examples and learns patterns.



## Example



Instead of writing:

**IF message contains MONEY → Spam**

We show the machine examples:

| Message | Answer |
|----------|----------|
| Win free money now | Spam |
| Claim your prize | Spam |
| Meeting at 3 PM | Not Spam |
| Bring your notebook tomorrow | Not Spam |



After seeing many examples:

🤔 The machine starts learning patterns on its own.

steps.title: Key Idea
steps.body:
✅ **Rule-Based AI learns from rules.**

✅ **Machine Learning learns from examples.**

✅ **Instead of telling the machine every rule, we show it examples and let it discover patterns.**

steps.title: 🚀 Your First AI Mission
steps.body:
In the last chapter, we learned that machines can learn from examples instead of following rules.

Now it's your turn to build a real AI system.

🎯 Your Mission

Build an AI that can recognise whether an image contains:

🐱 Cat
🐶 Dog

Just like a real AI engineer, you'll build this system step by step.

By the end of this chapter, you'll have trained your own AI model.

steps.title: 🤔 What does it need first?
steps.body:
But before an AI can learn...

🤔 What does it need first?

steps.title: 🤔 Every AI Needs Data
steps.body:
Imagine you meet someone who has never seen a cat before.

Could they recognise one?

Probably not.


First, they would need to see many examples.

Machines work the same way.


Before an AI can learn, it needs lots of examples.

These examples are called:

👉 Data

Examples of data include:

📷 Images

📝 Text

🔢 Numbers

🎤 Audio

steps.title: 🗂️ Choosing the Right Data
steps.body:
For our AI...

What kind of data do we need?

steps.title: 🐱 What Data Do We Need?
steps.body:
Our goal is to build an AI that recognises Cats and Dogs.

So what examples should we collect?

✅ Pictures of Cats

✅ Pictures of Dogs

The more good examples we collect, the more the AI can learn.

Let's collect our data.

steps.title: Guided Lab 1: Collect Your First AI Dataset
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to collect and upload data for an AI system.



## Problem

An AI cannot learn to recognise **cats** and **dogs** unless it has examples to learn from.

Your job is to collect the data.



## Instructions



**Step 1:**
Search online for **10 images of cats**.

**Step 2:**
Download all **10 cat images** to your computer.

**Step 3:**
Search online for **10 images of dogs**.

**Step 4:**
Download all **10 dog images** to your computer.

**Step 5:**
Open **ABITA**.

**Step 6:**
Go to the **Data Panel**.
If the left panel is hidden, use the floating icon to open it.

**Step 7:**
Click **Add Data**.

**Step 8:**
Upload all **20 images** into the **Data Bank**.

**Step 9:**
Check that all uploaded images appear in the **Data Bank**.




## Success Criteria


- ✅ 10 cat images downloaded
- ✅ 10 dog images downloaded
- ✅ All 20 images uploaded successfully
- ✅ All images appear in the Data Bank



## Expected Result


🤔 You have collected and uploaded data.

🤔 The AI still does not know which images are cats and which are dogs.

🤔 In the next lab, you will organise and label the data so it can be used for learning.

steps.title: 🤔 But There's One Problem...
steps.body:
We have collected 20 pictures.

But does the machine know which pictures are Cats...

and which are Dogs?

❌ No.

To the machine...

they are just pictures.

Someone has to tell the machine the correct answer.

This process is called...

Labelling.

steps.title: 🏷️ What Is a Label?
steps.body:
A Label is the correct answer we give to each example.

For example:

🐱 Cat Image → Label: Cat

🐶 Dog Image → Label: Dog




When data already has labels, it is called:

✅ Labelled Data



When data has no labels, it is called:

⚪ Unlabelled Data



Before an AI can learn, someone must create these labels.

Today...

You are that person. Let's label our dataset.

steps.title: Guided Lab 2: Create a Labelled Dataset
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to create a labelled dataset for training an AI model.



## Problem

You have already collected a dataset containing images of:

- 🐱 Cats
- 🐶 Dogs

The AI still cannot learn because it does not know what each image contains.

Your job is to organise the dataset by assigning the correct label to every image.



## Instructions

**Step 1:**
Open **ABITA**.



**Step 2:**
From the **Datasets** menu, drag a **Label Data** block onto the canvas.



**Step 3:**
Double-click the **Label Data** block to open its **Properties Panel**.


**Step 4:**
Create two labels:

- **Cat**
- **Dog**



**Step 5:**
Open the **Data Bank** tab.

If the left panel is hidden, use the floating icon to open it.



**Step 6:**
Select all **Cat** and **Dog** images from the **Data Bank**.



**Step 7:**
Drag the selected images into the **Items** section of the **Label Data** block.



**Step 8:**
Assign the correct label to every image.



**Step 9:**
Verify that every image has been labelled correctly.



**Step 10:**
Under **Save Dataset**, enter the dataset name:

**CatDog Labelled Dataset**



**Step 11:**
Click **💾 Save Dataset**.



**Step 12:**
Open the **Datasets** tab from the left panel.



**Step 13:**
Check that **CatDog Labelled Dataset** appears in the list of saved datasets.




## Success Criteria

- ✅ Two labels (**Cat** and **Dog**) have been created
- ✅ Every image has been labelled correctly
- ✅ The dataset is saved as **CatDog Labelled Dataset**
- ✅ **CatDog Labelled Dataset** appears in the **Datasets** tab




## Expected Result

🤔 Every image now has the correct label.

🤔 The dataset is organised and ready for **Supervised Learning**.

🤔 The AI can now learn because it knows the correct answer for every example.

👉 In the next lab, you will use **CatDog Labelled Dataset** to train your first AI model.

steps.title: Challenge Lab 1: Build a Lion and Tiger Dataset
steps.prompt:
## Problem

You are working as a **Data Labeler** for a wildlife technology company.

Your team is building a smart camera that can automatically recognise whether an animal is a **lion** or a **tiger**.

Before the AI can learn, it needs labelled data.

Your job is to collect and label the images that will be used to train the AI.



## Requirements

- Collect **10 images of lions**
- Collect **10 images of tigers**
- Upload all images into **ABITA**
- Create a **Label Data** block
- Create two labels:
  - Lion
  - Tiger
- Label all images correctly
- Save the dataset with the name:

**WildLifeTigerLion**



## Success Criteria

- ✅ A **Lion** label has been created
- ✅ A **Tiger** label has been created
- ✅ All images have been labelled correctly
- ✅ The dataset is saved as **WildLifeTigerLion**
- ✅ **WildLifeTigerLion** appears in the **Datasets** tab



## Expected Result

🤔 You have created a labelled dataset for a wildlife AI system.

🤔 Every image has been assigned the correct label.

🤔 The dataset is ready to be used for AI training.

steps.title: Challenge Lab 2: Build a Cars and Bikes Dataset
steps.prompt:
## Problem

You are working as a **Data Labeler** for a smart traffic management company.

Your team is building an AI system that can automatically recognise **cars** and **bikes** from traffic camera images.

Before the AI can learn, it needs labelled data.

Your job is to collect and label the images that will be used to train the AI.



## Requirements

- Collect **10 images of cars**
- Collect **10 images of bikes**
- Upload all images into **ABITA**
- Create a **Label Data** block
- Create two labels:
  - Car
  - Bike
- Label all images correctly
- Save the dataset with the name:

**TrafficCarBike**



## Success Criteria


- ✅ A **Car** label has been created
- ✅ A **Bike** label has been created
- ✅ All images have been labelled correctly
- ✅ The dataset is saved as **TrafficCarBike**
- ✅ **TrafficCarBike** appears in the **Datasets** tab



## Expected Result


🤔 You have created a labelled dataset for a traffic AI system.

🤔 Every image has been assigned the correct label.

🤔 The dataset is ready to be used for AI training.

steps.title: 🧑‍🏫 Now It's Time to Teach the AI
steps.body:
Excellent!

Let's see what we've done so far.

- ✅ We collected lots of images.
- ✅ We organised them into a labelled dataset.

Notice something important...

The AI already knows the **correct answer** for every example because **you provided the labels**.

When humans teach an AI using **labelled data**, this type of learning is called:

# 🎓 Supervised Learning

Today, **you are the teacher**.

Now it's time for the AI to start learning from your examples.

steps.title: 🧠 Training the AI
steps.body:
Every AI system starts with a Model.

At the beginning, the model knows nothing.

It has not learned any patterns yet.

We call this an:

🧠 Empty Model

When we give the model our labelled dataset, it begins studying every example.

It looks for patterns that help it tell Cats and Dogs apart.


This learning process is called: **🎓 Training**

During training, the model gradually learns from your examples.

steps.title: 🧠 A Trained Model
steps.body:
When training is complete...

the model has learned patterns from your labelled examples.

It is no longer an empty model.

It has now become a: 🧠 Trained Model

This trained model is now ready to recognise similar images.

It can use what it has learned to make predictions.

steps.title: Guided Lab 3: 🧠 Train Your First AI Model
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to train an AI model using a labelled dataset.



## Problem

You have already created a labelled dataset called:

**CatDog Labelled Dataset**

This dataset contains labelled images of:

- 🐱 Cats
- 🐶 Dogs

Now it is time to teach an AI model using this data.

In ABITA, you will use an **Image Supervised Model**.

It should also show the number of labels and images that were trained.

**CatDogModel**

title: 6. Testing Your First AI Model
subtitle: Learn how to test an AI model using new data.

steps.title: 🧑‍🏫 Why Do We Need Testing?
steps.body:
# 🎉 Congratulations!

You have successfully trained your first AI model.

Your model has learned patterns from your Training Data.

But before we celebrate...


We need to answer one important question.

🤔 Has the AI really learned?

How can we find out?

steps.title: 🤔 Can We Test Using the Same Images?
steps.body:
Imagine your teacher gives you 20 practice questions.

You memorise every answer.

Then, in the exam...

The teacher asks you exactly the same 20 questions.

Did you really learn...

or did you simply memorise the answers?

The same thing can happen with AI.

If we test the AI using the same images it learned from...

we cannot be sure whether it has really learned.

So what should we do instead?

steps.title: 🧪 Meet Test Data
steps.body:
Instead of using the same images again, we collect **new images** that the AI has **never seen before**.

These new images are called:

🧪 **Test Data**



Now we have **two different datasets**.

📚 **Training Data**

Used to **teach the AI**.

🧪 **Test Data**

Used to **check what the AI has learned**.


```text
📚 Training Data ─────► 🧠 Train the Model

🧪 Test Data ─────────► 🔍 Test the Model
```



| Dataset  Role (How will we use the data?) | Purpose |
|------------|----------|
| Training Data | Teach the AI |
| Test Data | Check if the AI learned |

Let's prepare our **Test Data**.

steps.title: Guided Lab 1: Create Your First Test Dataset
steps.instruction:
## Instructions

**Step 1:**
Download:

- 10 **new Cat images**
- 10 **new Dog images**

> **Important:** Do **not** use any images that were included in your **Training Data**.


**Step 2:**
Open **ABITA**.


**Step 3:**
Open the **Data Bank** tab.

If the left panel is hidden, use the floating icon to open it.


**Step 4:**
Upload all **20 new images** into the **Data Bank**.


**Step 5:**
From the **Datasets** menu, drag a **Label Data** block onto the canvas.


**Step 6:**
Double-click the **Label Data** block to open its **Properties Panel**.

Create two labels:

- **Cat**
- **Dog**


**Step 7:**
Select all **Cat** and **Dog** images from the **Data Bank**.

Drag them into the **Items** section.


**Step 8:**
Assign the correct label to every image.


**Step 9:**
In the **Save Dataset** section, enter the name:

**Test Data CatDog**


**Step 10:**
Click **Save Data**.


**Step 11:**
Open the **Datasets** tab from the left panel.


**Step 12:**
Check that **Test Data CatDog** appears in the list of saved datasets.

steps.title: 🔮 What Is a Prediction?
steps.body:
Your model has **never seen the Test Data before**.

Now it will look at each new image and make its **best guess**.

For example:

- 🐱 "I think this is a **Cat**."
- 🐶 "I think this is a **Dog**."

This guess is called a: 🔮 **Prediction**

Every time an AI gives an answer about **new data**, it is making a **prediction**.

Let's see how well your AI performs.

steps.title: ✅ Correct or Incorrect?
steps.body:
When the AI looks at a new image, it makes a Prediction.

We can then compare the AI's prediction with the Actual Label.

If they are the same...

✅ Correct Prediction

If they are different...

❌ Incorrect Prediction

Every prediction helps us understand how well the AI has learned.



** Example 1**

```text
🖼️ Image: Dog

🏷️ Actual Label: Dog

🤖 AI Prediction: Cat

❌ Incorrect Prediction
```


** Example 2**

```text
🖼️ Image: Cat

🏷️ Actual Label: Cat

🤖 AI Prediction: Cat

✅ Correct Prediction
```

steps.title: 📊 What Is Accuracy?
steps.body:
# 📊 What Is Accuracy?

Instead of checking **one prediction at a time**, we can measure **how many predictions were correct**.

This is called:

📊 **Accuracy**

# Example

```text
20 Test Images
        │
        ▼
18 Correct
        │
        ▼
2 Incorrect
        │
        ▼
Accuracy = 90%
```



| Images Tested | Correct Predictions | Accuracy |
|--------------|--------------------|----------|
| 4 | 4 | 100% |
| 4 | 3 | 75% |
| 4 | 2 | 50% |


The **higher the accuracy**, the **better the AI performed** on the **Test Data**.

steps.title: Guided Lab 2 – Test Your First AI Model
steps.instruction:
## Goal

Learn how to test an AI model using **Test Data** and understand its predictions and accuracy.



## Problem

You have already trained an AI model called:

**CatDogModel**

You have also prepared a dataset called:

**Test Data CatDog**

This dataset contains **new** labelled images of **Cats** and **Dogs** that the model has never seen before.

Your job is to test **CatDogModel** using **Test Data CatDog** and evaluate how well it performs.



## Instructions

**Step 1:**
Open **ABITA**.


**Step 2:**
From the **Models** tab, drag **CatDogModel** onto the canvas.


**Step 3:**
Open the **Datasets** tab.

If the left panel is hidden, use the floating icon to open it.


**Step 4:**
Locate:

**Test Data CatDog**



**Step 5:**
Drag from the **amber output node** on **Test Data CatDog** to the **amber input node** on **CatDogModel** to connect them.



**Step 6:**
Double-click **CatDogModel** and verify that it shows:

- ✅ Connected
- **Test Data CatDog**


**Step 7:**
If the model properties are not visible, double-click **CatDogModel** again.

Under **4 · Test**, click:

🚀 **Run Test**


**Step 8:**
Observe the testing progress.

You may see messages such as:

- Initialising model...
- Loading Test Data...
- Inferencing...
- Evaluating predictions...

During **Inferencing**, the model analyses each new image and makes its prediction.


**Step 9:**
Wait until the testing process is complete.


**Step 10:**
Observe the **Overall Accuracy** displayed by the model.

This tells you how many predictions were correct.


**Step 11:**
Click **📋 Expanded View**.


**Step 12:**
Review the results for every image.

For each image, observe:

- 🖼️ The image
- 🏷️ The Actual Label
- 🤖 The AI Prediction
- 📈 The Confidence Score
- ✅ Whether the prediction was **Correct** or ❌ **Incorrect**


**Step 13:**
Compare the **AI Prediction** with the **Actual Label**.

Notice which images were predicted correctly and which were not.



## Success Criteria

- ✅ **Test Data CatDog** is connected to **CatDogModel**
- ✅ The model successfully completes testing
- ✅ **Overall Accuracy** is displayed
- ✅ **Expanded View** opens successfully
- ✅ Predictions are displayed for every image
- ✅ Actual Labels, AI Predictions, Confidence Scores, and Results are visible



## Expected Result

🤔 The AI makes a prediction for every new image.

🤔 Each prediction is compared with the correct label.

🤔 The **Confidence Score** shows how confident the AI is about each prediction.

🤔 The **Overall Accuracy** shows how well the model performs on images it has never seen before.

🤔 Correct and incorrect predictions help us understand how well the AI has learned from the **Training Data**.

👉 In the next chapter, you will discover why AI models sometimes make mistakes and learn how to improve them.

steps.title: 🤔 Why Did the AI Make Mistakes?
steps.body:
Did your AI get **every prediction correct**?

Probably not.

Even well-trained AI models can make mistakes.

Think about why.

Was it because:

- There were **too few examples**?
- Some images were **blurry**?
- Cats and dogs looked **very similar**?
- The labels were **incorrect**?

Understanding mistakes is an important part of building **better AI**.

steps.title: 📈 How Confident Is the AI?
steps.body:
When the AI looks at a new image...

it doesn't just make a **Prediction**.

It also tells us **how confident** it is about that prediction.


For example:

🐱 Prediction: **Cat**

📈 Confidence: **98%**




This means:

👉 "I think this is a Cat, and I'm **98% sure**."




Now look at another example:

🐱 Prediction: **Cat**

📈 Confidence: **55%**

The AI still predicts **Cat**...

but this time it is **less certain**.




## 🌟 Remember

🔮 **Prediction** = The AI's answer.

📈 **Confidence** = How sure the AI is about that answer.

In the next step, we'll compare the AI's prediction with the correct answer to see whether it was right or wrong.

title: 7. Inside the AI's Brain
subtitle: Understanding How AI Learns, Makes Predictions, and Improves

steps.title: 🕵️ The AI Mystery
steps.body:
# 🎉 Congratulations!

You have now built your **first AI model!** 🥳

Along the way, you:

- ✅ Trained an AI model
- 🧪 Tested it with new images
- 🤖 Watched it make predictions
- 🎯 Saw some predictions that were correct
- ❌ Saw some predictions that were incorrect
- 📊 Even noticed predictions with **high confidence** that were still wrong

---

## 🤔 Something Interesting Happened...

If the AI was **so confident**...

**Why did it still make mistakes?**

How can an AI say it's **98% confident** and still be completely wrong?

---

# 🔍 Let's Investigate

To solve this mystery...

we first need to go **inside the AI's brain**.

Let's discover **what a model really is**, how it learns, and why it sometimes gets things wrong.

steps.title: 🤔 What Is the AI's Brain?
steps.body:
Every AI system has a **brain**.

In Artificial Intelligence, this brain is called a:

# 🤖 Model

---

## What is a Model?

A **model** is the part of the AI that **learns from examples**.

As it learns, it begins to recognize **patterns** in the data.

Later, when it sees something new, it uses those patterns to make a **prediction**.

---

## 💡 Think of it Like This

The **model doesn't memorize every example**.

Instead, it learns the **patterns** that many examples have in common.

Those learned patterns help it make decisions about things it has **never seen before**.

---

## Without a Model...

❌ The AI cannot learn.

❌ The AI cannot recognize patterns.

❌ The AI cannot make predictions.

The **model is the intelligence** behind every AI system.


```text
        🧠 AI System

      ┌───────────────┐
      │     MODEL     │
      │ (AI's Brain)  │
      └───────────────┘
             │
             ▼
      🔮 Makes Predictions

steps.title: 🧠 Every Model Starts Empty
steps.body:
# 🧠 An Empty Model

When a new AI model is created...

it knows **absolutely nothing**.

It has never seen a Cat.

It has never seen a Dog.

It hasn't learned **any patterns** yet.

---

## This is called an **🧠 Empty Model**

An empty model has **no knowledge** and **no experience**.

It cannot make useful predictions because it hasn't learned anything yet.

---

## 🎒 Just Like a New Student

Imagine it's the **first day of school**.

A new student hasn't learned the lessons yet.

They need examples, practice, and feedback before they can answer questions correctly.

An AI model learns in exactly the same way.

It starts empty...

and learns **step by step** from the examples you give it.


```text
Before Training

        🧠
   ┌───────────┐
   │           │
   │  Empty    │
   │  Model    │
   │           │
   └───────────┘

Knows:

❌ Cat

❌ Dog

❌ Apple

❌ Car

steps.title: 📚 Teaching the Model
steps.body:
So...

**How do we teach an AI model?**

Do we write thousands of rules?

**No.**

Instead...

we teach it by showing **lots of labelled examples**.



## 🏷️ Training Examples

🐱 Cat

🐶 Dog

🐱 Cat

🐶 Dog

🐱 Cat

🐶 Dog

Every example tells the model:

> **"This is a Cat."**  
> **"This is a Dog."**



## 🧠 During Training...

The model studies every example.

It compares them.

It looks for similarities and differences.

Slowly...

it begins discovering **patterns** that help it tell Cats and Dogs apart.

---

```text
      📚 Training Data

🐱   🐶   🐱   🐶   🐱

            │
            ▼

      🧠 Empty Model

            │
         Learning...
            │
            ▼

 🧠 Model Starts Learning Patterns
```

steps.title: 🤔 What Does the Model Remember?
steps.body:
Here's a question...

After training on **hundreds of Cat pictures**...

What do you think the model stores?

- A) Every Cat picture
- B) Important patterns



## ✅ The Correct Answer

**B) Important Patterns**

The model does **not** memorize every picture.

Instead...

it learns the **important patterns** that appear again and again across many examples.

These patterns help the model recognize **new images** it has never seen before.



## 💡 Why?

If the model tried to remember every picture...

it would struggle whenever it saw a different Cat.

By learning **patterns** instead of **pictures**, the model can recognize many different Cats and Dogs.



```diagram
Did the AI store...

🐱 🐱 🐱 🐱 🐱
🐱 🐱 🐱 🐱 🐱
🐱 🐱 🐱 🐱 🐱

        
      ❌ NO


Instead, it stores...

      🧩 Patterns
```

steps.title: 🧩 What Are Patterns?
steps.body:
Imagine someone shows you **hundreds of Cats**.

After a while...

you begin noticing things like:

- 👂 Pointy ears
- 〰️ Whiskers
- 👀 Eyes
- 🟤 Fur
- 😊 Face shape

You don't remember **every Cat** you have ever seen.

Instead...

your brain remembers these **patterns**.



## 🤖 AI Learns the Same Way

AI learns in a very similar way.

- Nobody tells the AI what these patterns are.
- During training, the model discovers them by itself.
- The more examples it sees, the better it becomes at recognizing those patterns.



```diagram
      Many Cat Images

🐱   🐱   🐱   🐱   🐱

            │
            ▼

     🧠 Model Learns

            │
            ▼

      👂 Pointy Ears
      👀 Eyes
      〰️ Whiskers
      😊 Face Shape
      🟤 Fur
```

steps.title: 🧠 Where Are These Patterns Stored?
steps.body:
So where do these patterns go?

The model has a **memory**.

But...

it doesn't store **pictures**.

It stores **patterns**.



## 🔢 How Does It Store Patterns?

Inside the model, these patterns are stored as **millions (or even billions) of tiny numbers**.

Every time the model learns from another example...

those numbers change a little.

Over time...

they become better at representing useful patterns.



## 💡 The Better the Training...

- Better examples
- Better learning
- Better patterns
- Better predictions


```diagram
During Training

🖼 Images
   │
   ▼
🧩 Model finds patterns
   │
   ▼
🔢 Patterns become numbers
   │
   ▼
┌────────────────────┐
│      🧠 MODEL       
│                               
│  🔢 🔢 🔢 🔢 🔢  
│  Learned patterns  
│  stored as numbers 
└────────────────────┘

steps.title: 🎯 How Does the Model Make a Prediction?
steps.body:
Now imagine we show the model a **brand new picture**.

It has **never seen this image before**.



## 🔍 What Happens Next?

The model compares the new image...

with the **patterns** it learned during training.

Then it asks:

**Which label matches these patterns the best?**

- 🐱 Cat?
- 🐶 Dog?

Finally...

the model chooses the **best match**.

This is called a **Prediction**.



## 📊 Along with the Prediction...

The model also tells us **how confident** it is about its answer.

For example:

- 🐱 Prediction: **Cat**
- 📈 Confidence: **96%**



```diagram
       🖼️ New Image
             │
             ▼
         🧠 AI Model
             │
   Compare with Learned
        🧩 Patterns
             │
             ▼
      🐱 Prediction
             │
             ▼
   📈 Confidence: 96%
```

steps.title: 🤔 Why Do Models Make Mistakes?
steps.body:
Now we can finally solve our mystery.

If the model learns patterns...

why does it still make mistakes?

There are **four common reasons**.

---

## 1️⃣ Not Enough Examples

The model hasn't seen enough examples during training.

Just like a student who studies only one chapter before an exam...

the model hasn't learned enough to recognise every situation.

---

## 2️⃣ Poor Quality or Wrongly Labelled Data

The training data may contain problems.

For example:

🖼️ Blurry or unclear images.

🏷️ A Cat image labelled as **Dog**.

🏷️ A Dog image labelled as **Cat**.

When the labels are wrong...

the model learns the wrong patterns.

👉 Bad data teaches bad lessons.

---

## 3️⃣ Difficult Examples

Some images are naturally difficult.

For example...

🐱 Some Cats look very similar to Dogs.

Even humans sometimes get confused.

If it is difficult for people...

it can also be difficult for AI.

---

## 4️⃣ The Model Hasn't Learned Enough Patterns

Sometimes the model simply hasn't discovered enough useful patterns yet.

Maybe it focused on the wrong details.

Or maybe it needs more training.

As a result...

it makes the wrong prediction.

---

🌍 Even the world's best AI models sometimes make mistakes.

Making mistakes is a normal part of learning.

```diagram

                    ❌ Model Made a Mistake
                              │
                              ▼
                     🤔 What Happened?
                              │
     ┌────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┐
     │                        │                        │
     ▼                        ▼                        ▼
① Not Enough           ② Poor or Wrongly        ③ Difficult
   Examples               Labelled Data            Examples
     │                        │                        │
     ▼                        ▼                        ▼
Didn't see             Wrong labels or         Cats and Dogs
enough examples        blurry images           look very similar

                              │
                              ▼
                    ④ Weak Pattern Learning
                              │
                              ▼
              The model hasn't learned enough
                   useful patterns yet.

steps.title: 🚀 How Do We Improve an AI Model?
steps.body:
When an AI makes mistakes...

engineers don't throw it away.

Instead...

they improve it.



## 🛠️ How Can We Improve It?

- 📚 Collect more training data.
- 🗑️ Remove poor-quality or confusing images.
- 🏷️ Correct incorrect labels.
- 🔄 Train the model again.
- ⚙️ Use better training methods or a more advanced model.



## 🌱 Every Improvement Matters

Each improvement helps the model learn **better patterns**.

Better patterns lead to:

- ✅ Better predictions
- 📈 Higher accuracy
- ❌ Fewer mistakes



## 🌍 This Is How Real AI Systems Improve

Real AI systems are **never finished**.

Engineers continuously collect better data, improve training, and retrain their models.

This cycle of **learning → testing → improving** is how AI becomes smarter over time.



```diagram
                                
                  📚 Data
                       │
                       ▼
               🧠 Train Model
                       │
                       ▼
                🧪 Test Model
                       │
                       ▼
              ❌ Mistakes Found
                       │
                       ▼
                🔧 Improve AI
                       │
      ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
      ▼                ▼                ▼
 📚 Better Data   ⚙️ Better Training   🧠 Better Model
      │                │                │
      └────────────────┼────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
                🧠 Train Again
                       │
                       └──────► Repeat

steps.title: 🎉 Chapter Summary
steps.body:
Today, you opened the **AI's brain**.

Now you know that...


- 🧠 A **model** is the brain of an AI system.
- 📚 Models learn from **examples**.
- 🧩 Models discover **patterns** in the data.
- 🔢 Those patterns are stored as **millions of tiny numbers**.
- 🎯 Models use those patterns to make **predictions**.
- ❌ Models can still make **mistakes**.
- 🔧 Better data and better training help models become more accurate.

---



# 💡 Remember

> **AI isn't intelligent because it knows every answer.**

> **AI is intelligent because it learns useful patterns from data.**

---



# 🚀 What's Next?

In the next chapter...

you'll become an **AI engineer**.

You'll improve your own AI model by:

- 📚 Collecting better training data
- 🏷️ Fixing incorrect labels
- 🧹 Removing poor-quality data
- 🔄 Training the model again

Just like engineers do when building real-world AI systems.
